coredata.dc:title: Superparamagnetic nanocomposite Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs as fluorescent probe for copper (II) detection
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Shuling
Wang, Shengnan
Wang, Xiuling
Zhai, Linzhi
coredata.dc:description: A new type of magnetic fluorescent nanocomposite (Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs) was prepared by bonding carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 nanocomposites through amine-carbonyl interactions. The results showed that the prepared nanocomposites have spherical structure with good dispersion in aqueous solution, superparamagnetic and good fluorescence properties. In addition, the Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs nanocomposites were used as a fluorescent probe for the first time to detect Cu2+, and exhibited high sensitive and selective detection properties in a wide linear range of 0–80 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ was 0.16 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic materials
Nanocomposites
Fluorescence
Carbon quantum dots
Copper ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs by Cu2+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR Spectra (A) and XRD pattern (B) of as-prepared samples. SEM(C and D) and TEM (E and F) images of the Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs nanocomposites.
(A) Magnetization curves of synthesized nanoparticles, the insert shows the magnetic separation of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs.

(A) Linear relationship between F0/F and the concentrations of Cu2+. (The inset figure: Fluorescence spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ (interval 10 μM). (B) Selectivity research of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs for Cu2+ (the concentration of all metal ions: 40 μM). (C) The XPS spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs-Cu (II) (after interaction with Cu2+). (D) Cu2p XPS spectra of CuSO4 and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs-Cu (II).

head.ce:title: Superparamagnetic nanocomposite Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs as fluorescent probe for copper (II) detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Biology and Materials Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou 215000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Biology and Materials Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Biology and Materials Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou 215000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, No. 2 Mengxi Road, Zhenjiang 212000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, No. 2 Mengxi Road, Zhenjiang 212000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new type of magnetic fluorescent nanocomposite (Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs) was prepared by bonding carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 nanocomposites through amine-carbonyl interactions. The results showed that the prepared nanocomposites have spherical structure with good dispersion in aqueous solution, superparamagnetic and good fluorescence properties. In addition, the Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs nanocomposites were used as a fluorescent probe for the first time to detect Cu2+, and exhibited high sensitive and selective detection properties in a wide linear range of 0–80 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ was 0.16 μM.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decade, fluorescent nanomaterials have been very active in the detection of heavy metals. Especially heavy metal sensors based on semiconductor quantum dots [1,2], precious metal nanoparticles [3–5], and carbon-based nanomaterials [6] have been widely developed. However, these sensors were limited in their application due to their toxicity, cost, and difficulty in recycling. In recent years, magnetic fluorescent bifunctional nanocomposites have received extensive attention due to their low-cost, clean, and separable [7,8]. Among magnetic materials, magnetite (Fe3O4) nanoparticles were widely used in many fields because of their low toxicity, raw material economy, easy synthesis and easy functionalization. On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with outstanding performance are also unique in the field of fluorescent materials (such as low cytotoxicity, economical raw materials, biocompatibility and easy synthesis). So far, the application of bifunctional nanocomposites based on Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) and CQDs in other fields has been reported [9,10]. However, as far as we know, there is no report for synthesis of Fe3O4/C-dots nanocomposites as a Cu2+ sensor.

In this work, we successfully synthesized a new type of magnetic fluorescent nanocomposites (Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs), which were used as sensor material for the recognition of Cu2+. Compared with other metal ions, the selectivity and sensitivity to Cu2+ were found to be excellent by the fluorescence phenomenon. In addition, the magnetic fluorescent nanocomposites can be magnetically separated by an external magnetic field to avoid contamination of the analytical sample.

The details of chemicals, characterization techniques, CQDs’ and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2′ preparation were discussed in “Electronic Supplementary Material (ESM)”. In the connection process of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 and CQDs, we found suitable connection conditions by adjusting the four experimental parameters of pH, buffer solution, temperature and reaction time. Briefly, 0.1 g of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 nanoparticles was dispersed in phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.4), followed by the sonication for 20 min. Then, EDC/NHS (50 mM/25 mM) and CQDs solution (30 mL) were added. After reaction for 24 h at 37 °C in a reciprocating oscillator, the Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs nanocomposite was obtained. In order to obtain products with higher purity, uniform particles and easy dispersion in water, we only collected the products with smaller particles suspended in the reaction solution. Three preparation experiments were carried out and the Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs yield after separation was 55%. The synthesis process was shown in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs by Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: For Cu2+ detection, CuSO4 (1 mM, H2O) solution was added to 3 mL Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs sensing system (1.5 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs in PBS, pH 7.4) to obtain the designed Cu2+ concentration range (0–80 μM). For selective research, 40 μM of different metals were respectively added to 3 mL Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs sensing system. All the fluorescence spectra were carried out under the same conditions. The quenching constant (Ksv) was calculated by the Stern-Volmer equation, F0/F = 1 + KsvC, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the sample in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively, C is the concentration of Cu2+ ions.

The FTIR spectra data of CQDs, Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs were displayed in Fig. 1 A. The spectrum of CQDs showed the peaks at 1636 and 1426 cm−1, which were associated with carboxylic acid bond (–COO–). For Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the characteristic Fe-O peak was observed at 582 cm−1. Also, the presence of broad band at 3432 cm−1 and intense band at 1622 cm−1 depicted –OH stretching vibrations on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Compared with the spectrums of CQDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the new peaks at 1057 cm−1and 799 cm−1 were observed in the spectrum of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs, which were assigned to the stretching vibrations of (Si-O–Si) and (Si–O–Fe), respectively. In addition, bands at 1634, 1501 and 1257 cm−1 were attributed to vibration of -C = O, –N–H and -C-N. In conclusion, the characteristic peaks of –NH-CO- and the shift of the Fe-O bond peak (from 582 cm−1 in bare Fe3O4 to 597 cm−1 in Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs nanocomposite) implied that the CQDs have been successfully attached to the Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 nano-composites. FTIR proved that CQDs and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 was combined through an amine-carbonyl interaction, which can make a large number of tiny CQDs modify on the surface of Fe3O4 to inhibit the accumulation of Fe3O4.

The crystalline structures of the as-prepared Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 were characterized by XRD (Fig. 1B). The characteristic diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 were clearly corresponded to the cubic spinel structure of Fe3O4 (JCPDS 19–0629) [11]. There was not any impurity peaks were detected.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) showed the uniform spherical structure of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs nanocomposites with a diameter ~ 45 nm (Fig. 1C-E). And it showed good dispersion in aqueous solution. It can be observed that a large amount of CQDs were deposited on the surface of the nanoparticles (Fig. 1D). In the Fig. 1F, the interplanar spacings of 0.250 nm and 0.329 nm were clearly measured, which correspond to the lattice planes (311) of Fe3O4 and the (006) lattice planes of CQDs [12], respectively. These lattice spacing data demonstrate the successful combination of CQDs with Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 nanocomposite.

The magnetic properties were analyzed using vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) technique at room temperature. The magnetic hysteresis loops of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs were shown in Fig. 2 A. The samples showed superparamagnetism without remnant magnetization and coercive force, and their saturation magnetization intensities were 56.55, 42.31, 33.19 and 19.98 emu·g−1, respectively. The decrease of saturation magnetization may be due to the formation of continuous anti-magnetosphere (amino silicon layer and CQDs) on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs nanocomposites can still achieve complete magnetic separation by placing magnets outside the container (inset photograph of Fig. 2A).

Fig. 2B showed the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs. The maximum emission peak of pure CQDs was at 442 nm, and occurred red-shifted by 8 nm after being connected with magnetic nanoparticles because of changes in the microenvironment. The fluorescence microscopy images of CQDs and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs showed intense fluorescence under the UV lamp (Fig. S2). These phenomena further confirmed the good combination of CQDs with the Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 nanoparticles.

The fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs sensing system at 450 nm gradually decreased as the Cu2+ added into the system from 0 to 80 μM (insert, Fig. 3 A). The quenching efficiency (F0/F) of the nanocomposites exhibited a nice linear correlation (R2 = 0.9935) with the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0 to 80 μM (Fig. 3A), followed the Stern-Volmer equation by a static mechanism. It showed a good reproducibility with a 0.52% relative standard deviation (RSD), through three parallel determinations at a sample containing 10.00 μM Cu2+. The limit of detection for Cu2+ was calculated to be 0.16 µM (S/N = 3), which was much lower than the permissible limit (15.4 µM) of Cu2+ recommended by the World Health Organization for drinking water [11]. It was also lower than the values previously reported (Table S1).

In order to further study the selectivity of fluorescent probe for Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of nanocomposites in the presence of other metal ions (such as: Mn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ag2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mg2+) were determined under the same conditions. The presence of Cu2+ led to a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of composites (Fig. 3B). After adding Co2+ or Ni2+, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly because of their similar ionic radii size to Cu2+ [13]. In contrast, no significant quenching effect was observed when other metal ions were added. On these grounds, the method used not only has high sensitivity, but also exhibits high selectivity towards Cu2+.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was allowed to investigate the binding model between Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs and Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 3C, the Si2p, C1s, N1s, O1s and Fe2p peaks appeared in Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs. The high-resolution scan of C1s, O1s, N1s and Fe2p were shown in Fig. s3. All the graphical results further confirmed the structure of the Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs nanocomposites. After interacting with Cu2+, Cu2p peak appeared in the XPS diagram of Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs-Cu (II). As can be seen in Fig. 3D, the binding energy of Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 were observed at 935.2, 955.0 in CuSO4 (copper salts that provide Cu2+ in the experiment) and shifted to 933.8, 953.6 eV in Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs-Cu (II), respectively. The shift of binding energy proved an electron transfer process from numerous active sites in Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs (such as N atoms and –OH groups) to Cu2+, which could lead to an efficient fluorescence quenching. [14].

In summary, Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs nanocomposite was successfully prepared, and exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity (LOD = 0.16 μM) for Cu2+ as a fluorescent probe. After testing, the nanocomposites can be quickly recovered by magnetic separation. To the best of our knowledge, Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2/CQDs nanocomposite has not been used for the fluorescence detection of Cu2+. This work, multifunctional materials with magnetism, fluorescence and other properties were used for the determination of Cu2+, which provides a new direction for the detection of ion in biological separation and sensing.

Shuling Dong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Shengnan Wang: Validation, Formal analysis. Xiuling Wang: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Linzhi Zhai: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 411732302) and Natural Science Fund Project of Colleges in Jiangsu Province (13KJB150035).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.128404.
